in one place.

Track weekly cargo contribution by port pair, break down cargo cost per unit, and analyse vessel slot economics — slot costs, bunker history, voyage simulation and a slot-cost calculator. Pick a module to begin.

Cost per Unit

Search a port pair to see its cost per unit broken down by component — agent, terminal, haulage, slot and fixed. Each pair uses its own latest week.

Open module →

Detect suspicious cost components — high/low vs peers, placeholder values, unusual presence, negatives and week-over-week jumps — across the latest 10 weeks.

Turn a voyage simulation into a slot cost — enter charter hire, port charges and editable bunker prices, then copy an Excel-ready summary.

Vessel Slot Cost

View is grouped by trade region. Sliders set standard-region prices ($/MT); WAT-region vessels shift by the same dollar amount from their own default. At default ($700 / $600 / $1100) every vessel matches the workbook.

Slot cost by vessel

Bunker price sensitivity

Each line = one vessel's slot cost as all three fuel prices move together from −50% to +50% of its region default. The 0% point is the workbook baseline.

Service rotation

Detail — slot cost & cost breakdown

Tip: click → Calc on any vessel to open it in the Slot Cost Calculator with its full cost detail.
ServiceVesselCapacity (TEU)DaysBunker costTotal op. costSlot (default)Slot (current)Δ vs defaultAction
Slot cost = Total operating cost ÷ effective capacity. Rows grouped by trade region. Bunker cost flexes with each vessel's own fuel mix; charter hire, port and misc held constant. Click a header to sort.

Select a vessel · click a card to see its previous bunker studies

Wildcard search across every study's rotation string and remark — match ports, partners, congestion notes, monsoon flags, etc.

BSA = suggested Bunker Slot Allocation (based on 14T). Click a header to sort.
Live Voyage Schedule Simulator — hosted on your GitHub (forked from LeahLiuL/Schedule-Simulation). After running a simulation, send the fuel results to the Slot Cost Calculator. Open in new tab ↗
Loading the voyage simulator…
This loads the in-house simulator hosted on your GitHub. First load may take a few seconds.
How the slot cost is calculated
Slot cost = (Charter hire + Bunker + Port charges + Misc) ÷ effective capacity.
Bunker = Σ (fuel MT × price) across LSFO, HSFO and MGO. Charter hire = daily rate × round-trip days.

Market vs Daily basis (on the Vessel Slot Cost page): Market (Weekly PL) uses the full weekly charter cost; Daily (Adjusted PL) uses the adjusted daily-equivalent rate. Same formula, different charter input — both reconcile to the source workbook.

Monsoon scenarios (Bunker History) reflect higher fuel burn and longer round-trip days during monsoon season versus the no-monsoon baseline.

Per FEU in the Excel summary = per-TEU slot cost × 2 (one FEU = two TEU).

Composition
Slot cost = (Charter hire + Bunker + Port charges + Misc) ÷ effective capacity. Bunker = Σ (fuel MT × price). Same formula as the Vessel Slot Cost page.

3 · Bunker price sensitivity

Slot cost vs the absolute baseline fuel price ($/MT). Baseline is HSFO if the vessel burns HSFO, otherwise LSFO; the other fuels scale proportionally. The marked point is the current price.

3 · Charter-hire sensitivity

Slot cost vs the absolute charter-hire daily rate, in $2,500 steps. The marked point is the current rate.

4 · Excel-ready summary · mirrors your Voyage inputs — add a column to compare scenarios

Copied — paste into Excel ✓
R/T Total Cost = Bunker + Vessel Charter + Port&Misc. Slot Cost (Per FEU) = R/T Total ÷ Declared Capacity × 2 (FEU = 2 TEU), rounded. The first column mirrors your Voyage inputs above; “Sync from Voyage inputs” refreshes it. Add columns to compare alternative scenarios (e.g. monsoon). White cells are editable; “Copy table for Excel” puts a tab-separated grid on your clipboard.
Per-unit contribution margin (contribution ÷ units) by port pair, split into 20′ and 40′ size types, for the selected revenue week. Bookings carrying both 20′ and 40′ on one B/L are excluded (not granular enough to attribute). Source: Contribution_by_Booking.xlsx.
How contribution margin is defined
Contribution = Total cargo revenue − Total cargo cost, per booking (B/L), as computed in the source workbook (CBP logic).

Per-unit margin for a port-pair × size cell = Σ contribution ÷ Σ units, where units = number of 20′ containers (for the 20′ bucket) or 40′ containers (for the 40′ bucket).

Size type comes from the booking's container counts: a B/L with only 20′ units goes to the 20′ bucket, only 40′ to the 40′ bucket. Mixed B/Ls (both 20′ and 40′) are excluded.

Cost basis: Incl. doc costs uses Total Contribution; Excl. doc costs strips document-related revenue and cost. Port pair: POL→POD is the ocean leg; POR→DEL is door-to-door origin/destination.

Contribution per unit by port pair & size

Port pair 20′ units 20′ contrib / unit 20′ total contrib 40′ units 40′ contrib / unit 40′ total contrib Blended / unit
“Contrib / unit” = total contribution for that size ÷ number of units of that size. “Blended / unit” = (20′ + 40′ total contribution) ÷ (20′ + 40′ units). Click a header to sort. Negative margins shown in red.
Search a port pair to understand its cost per unit and what drives it. Each pair uses its own most recent week of data (20′ and 40′ may each be from a different week, shown on the card). Cost is broken into its seven components, worked back from revenue − contribution. Mixed 20′/40′ B/Ls are excluded. Source: Contribution_by_Booking.xlsx.
How cost per unit is derived
Total cost = Total revenue − Contribution (per the source workbook). It equals the sum of seven components: Agent Commission, Laden Terminal, Laden Haulage, Internal Slot Cost, Empty Terminal, Empty Haulage and Container Fixed Cost.

Cost / unit for a port-pair card = Σ cost ÷ Σ units (units = 20′ or 40′ container count depending on the size filter). Each component is shown per unit and as a % of total cost.

Incl. vs excl. doc costs switches between the two cost bases; POL→POD is the ocean leg, POR→DEL door-to-door.
No port pairs match — try another week or search term.
Port-pair × size cells where contribution per unit is below zero — i.e. cost exceeded revenue — for the selected week, ranked most-negative first. Cells under 10 units are flagged low-confidence. Source: Contribution_by_Booking.xlsx (latest 10 weeks).

Negative contribution cells

Port pairSizeUnitsB/Ls Revenue / unitCost / unitContribution / unitFlags
Contribution / unit = revenue/unit − cost/unit. Negative (red) means the cargo lost money per box that week. ⚠ marks low-confidence cells (< 10 units). Click a header to sort.
Per-component cost anomalies vs peer port pairs (same POL or same POD/DEL). “Wobbles” = how many typical-deviations away from the peer median; raise the slider to flag only the most extreme. Comparisons use cells ≥10 units; thinner cells are flagged low-confidence. Source: Contribution_by_Booking.xlsx (latest 10 weeks).
How anomalies are detected
Each cost component is compared per unit against peer port pairs that share a leg (same POL, or same POD/DEL), within the selected week.

High / Low — uses the robust “MAD” method: find the peer median, measure the typical wobble around it, and flag values more than N wobbles away and materially different in dollars (so trivial cent-level differences aren't flagged).
Unusual presence / missing — a component is charged here but peers are ~$0 (or it's $0 here but peers all charge it).
Placeholder — a booking with a sentinel value (9999 / 99999 / 999 family) or a suspiciously round giant (≥$10,000 exact).
Negative cost — a component below $0, flagged only when peers are mostly positive (genuine cost-sharing credits are left alone).
Week-over-week jump — the component per unit changed sharply versus the same pair's earlier weeks.

Flagged cost components

Port pairSizeComponentType Value / unitPeer medianSeverityUnitsNotes
“Severity” = wobbles from peer median (for high/low), or × peer median (for presence/jumps). ⚠ = low-confidence (thin units or small peer group). Click a header to sort.
Trade analysis by Booking Trunk VVD — volume, revenue and contribution broken down across POL, destination, booking office, shipper and consignee for the selected week. Pick a Trunk VVD or “All”. Source: Contribution_by_Booking.xlsx (latest 10 weeks).

Contribution by POL

Top values by contribution for the selected dimension. Bars coloured green for positive CM, red for negative. Switch the dimension tabs above to re-chart.

By POL

Value UnitsTEU RevenueRev / unit CMCM / unitCM % neg B/L
CM % = contribution ÷ revenue. Negative CM rows shown in red. “neg B/L” = count of loss-making bookings in that group. Click a header to sort, or use “Rank by”.
